label: verify-view-water-agency-v-verjil
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …the others cited the constitutional text directly.

**Theme E — Bighorn forecloses the "not a property-related fee" fallback (5 of 7).**
Five panelists (all at 70) reasoned the district's threshold argument fails under *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil*, forcing the case to be decided on cost-of-service merits. The two higher-probability panelists did not include this in their rationales (one cited *Bighorn* only in authorities; one cited it for a different propositio…

[tetlock] …II D, section 6(b) at all, or could it instead be classified as a tax requiring Proposition 26/218 voter approval, or fall under some other exemption (e.g., tied to Water Code §375 conservation-rate authority)? (P=85)
- Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that volumetric, metered charges for ongoing retail water delivery to a parcel are 'fees for a property related service' under Art. XIII D §6(b)(4) — this is the closest controlling precedent …

[dialectic] …d of first because it is the weakest part of the defense and, if defeated early, forces the District onto the merits where it is most exposed.

- **Retail water delivery charges are the paradigm property-related fee.** *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that charges for ongoing water delivery service, imposed as an incident of property ownership, are property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, § 6 — notwithstanding that they are usage/m…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, 46 Cal.Rptr.3d 73, 138 P.3d 220","note":"Confirmed: real CA Supreme Court decision (July 24, 2006), correctly cited at 39 Cal.4th 205. The Court held that domestic water delivery through a pipeline is a \"property related service\" and that charges for it — including volumetric/metered charges — are \"fees\" or \"charges\" under Article XIII D, §6, notwithstanding usage-based billing; this squarely supports the snippets' use of Bighorn to foreclose a \"not property-related\" threshold argument and to establish that metered retail water delivery charges are property-related fees subject to §6(b).","source_urls":["https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/2631763/bighorn-desert-view-water-agency-v-verjil/","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/bighorn-desert-etc-v-verjil-33651","https://www.lawpipe.com/California/Bighorn-Desert_View_Water_Agency_v_Verjil.html"]}

data.canonical_cite: Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205, 46 Cal.Rptr.3d 73, 138 P.3d 220
data.note: Confirmed: real CA Supreme Court decision (July 24, 2006), correctly cited at 39 Cal.4th 205. The Court held that domestic water delivery through a pipeline is a "property related service" and that charges for it — including volumetric/metered charges — are "fees" or "charges" under Article XIII D, §6, notwithstanding usage-based billing; this squarely supports the snippets' use of Bighorn to foreclose a "not property-related" threshold argument and to establish that metered retail water delivery charges are property-related fees subject to §6(b).
